english: Hazelnut tea with milk reduced sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific hazelnut-flavoured or less-sugar variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages with milk. No hazelnut-flavoured or reduced-sugar sub-code exists; closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for hazelnut milk tea found; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is one medium cup of approximately 250 ml, equivalent to 250 g, as commonly served at Singaporean bubble tea and specialty tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Hazelnut Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hazelnut Milk Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010045, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hazelnut milk tea (less sugar). GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened milk tea / tea with sugar and milk (GI ~60–65), which is the closest approved analogous beverage. The 'less sugar' formulation reduces sugar load but does not substantially alter GI category given the remaining 6.72 g/100 g sugar and non-dairy creamer base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (62) × carbohydrate per serving (30.2 g from 250 g × 12.09/100) / 100 = 18.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.72 g sugar/100 g exceeding the 5 g/100 g SSB gate; added sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage as the 6.72 g/100 g sugar content classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance; only a genuinely unsweetened (0 g sugar) version would be acceptable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (6.72 g/100 g) in liquid form with rapid glycaemic absorption; 'less sugar' SSB variants are not exempt from the diabetes SSB hard gate
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this drink as it contains 6.72 g/100 g of added sugar in liquid form — which is absorbed rapidly and causes acute glycaemic excursions — and the 'less sugar' designation does not exempt it from the SSB restriction; requesting 0% sugar with no hazelnut syrup is the only diabetes-safe modification.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.30 g/100 g) from non-dairy creamer and added sugar (6.72 g/100 g) both independently elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage due to the presence of trans fat (~0.30 g/100 g) from non-dairy creamer — which raises LDL and lowers HDL cholesterol — combined with added sugar that promotes hypertriglyceridaemia; substituting fresh low-fat milk and requesting 0% sugar would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat from non-dairy creamer is pro-inflammatory; added sugar promotes inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as trans fat (~0.30 g/100 g) from non-dairy creamer is a hard disqualifier due to its strongly pro-inflammatory effect, and added sugar (6.72 g/100 g) further promotes inflammatory mediators; replacing non-dairy creamer with fresh low-fat milk and requesting 0% sugar is the recommended safer alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hazelnut Milk Tea (Less Sugar) is a freshly prepared Singaporean specialty tea beverage made by combining brewed black tea with non-dairy creamer and hazelnut-flavoured syrup, served at a reduced sugar level compared to the standard formulation. The drink is commonly available at bubble tea shops and specialty tea stalls across Singapore. It has a creamy, mildly sweet flavour profile with a distinct hazelnut aroma, a smooth texture from the non-dairy creamer, and a light tea bitterness that balances the sweetness. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, this beverage still contains 6.72 g of sugar per 100 g and 12.09 g of total carbohydrate per 100 g, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both diabetes and hypertension clinical gate criteria. The non-dairy creamer base contributes approximately 2.04 g of fat per 100 g, including an estimated 0.30 g of trans fat from partially hydrogenated palm oil, which is clinically significant for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis risk. Energy density is moderate at 67 kcal per 100 g. Sodium is low at 18 mg per 100 g. Dietary fibre is 1.02 g per 100 g, insufficient to meaningfully modulate glucose absorption from the sugar content. This beverage is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis in its standard less-sugar form. A 0% sugar, fresh-milk substitution version would substantially improve its clinical profile. Per a standard 250 ml serving, this drink delivers approximately 167.5 kcal, 30.2 g carbohydrate, and 16.8 g sugar.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010045) with a canonical confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea) and documented accordingly.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 1.70 g/100 g based on non-dairy creamer composition typical of Singapore milk tea formulations (palm-oil-based creamer); HPB record does not separately report saturated fat — value is an approximation. Trans fat estimated at 0.30 g/100 g from non-dairy creamer; HPB record does not separately report trans fat. Both values are flagged as estimated. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid breakdown, and moisture data for this freshly prepared specialty tea beverage are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. Protein_g (0.07 g/100 g) is near-zero and reflects the negligible protein contribution from trace amounts of non-dairy creamer and tea solids; this is not a zero-macro situation but a confirmed near-zero value from the HPB source. 4. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for sweetened milk tea; no direct GI measurement for hazelnut milk tea (less sugar) exists in any approved source. 5. Dietary tags 'plant_based' from the input record are retained with caution — non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives, making strict plant-based classification uncertain. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301099 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are closest defensible matches; no exact ontology codes exist for hazelnut-flavoured reduced-sugar milk tea.
